title: 🎙️ Indigenous Lands & Innovation
description: Today we explore how Cariboo and BC Indigenous nations are using technology on their own terms — for language revitalization, land stewardship, cultural preservation, and self-determined governance. We're looking for stories that centre Indigenous voices and leadership in innovation, where technology serves community sovereignty rather than replacing it. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today we explore how Cariboo and BC Indigenous nations are using technology on their own terms — for language revitalization, land stewardship, cultural preservation, and self-determined governance. We're looking for stories that centre Indigenous voices and leadership in innovation, where technology serves community sovereignty rather than replacing it.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt:
Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Indigenous Lands & Innovation' — Indigenous nations using technology, governance, and stewardship on their own terms.

CALIBRATION — use this ladder. Indigenous-led journalism on governance, rights, and self-determination should score in the moderate-to-good range even without an explicit technology angle — do not collapse everything that lacks a 'tech' hook to near 0:
- 70-100: Cariboo-specific stories bridging Indigenous governance/culture AND technology — Tŝilhqot'in/Secwépemc/Dakelh language revitalization tech, guardian programs using sensors/drones, GIS land mapping, Indigenous-led connectivity, digital cultural heritage projects.
- 45-69: Pan-Canadian Indigenous governance, sovereignty, land rights, treaty, or reconciliation journalism — APTN, Eagle Feather News, or IndigiNews-style coverage of First Nations governance, MMIWG, land claims, self-determination, or Indigenous-led services — even without a technology angle, per the curator's standing interest in Indigenous governance and sovereignty.
- 25-44: Indigenous cultural, community, or human-interest stories (awards, events, ceremonies) with limited governance or tech substance, or BC land-stewardship/environmental stories with meaningful First Nations involvement.
- 10-24: General BC land, environmental, or forestry stories that mention Indigenous communities only in passing.
- 0-9: No Indigenous angle at all.

Strong fits include: language revitalization technology for Tŝilhqot'in/Secwépemc/Dakelh, Indigenous guardian programs using remote monitoring and sensors, land management tech and environmental monitoring on Indigenous lands, digital tools for traditional knowledge preservation, community mapping and GIS for land claims, Indigenous-led connectivity projects, technology for cultural heritage documentation, mobile apps for language learning, drone/satellite monitoring for stewardship, reconciliation through digital collaboration, how Cariboo Indigenous nations use innovation for self-determination, and Indigenous governance, sovereignty, treaty, and land-rights journalism from any Canadian nation. Prefer articles that bridge Indigenous governance/culture AND technology, but governance/sovereignty journalism alone is a solid fit. Penalise heavily (score 0–9): international geopolitical and war news with no Indigenous rights angle; general wildfire and BC Wildlife Service updates not led by or directly impacting Indigenous communities or guardians; provincial forestry and environmental policy without explicit First Nations involvement; consumer tech and gadget reviews; science discoveries without Indigenous stewardship or sovereignty context; crime and judicial news unrelated to Indigenous rights. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] A tiny universe in a bottle reveals clues to the origins of life
items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

Researchers have created cosmic dust from scratch by recreating space-like conditions inside glass tubes. The dust contains complex carbon-rich molecules built from elements essential to life and produces infrared signals similar to real material found in space. By studying these laboratory samples, scientists can explore how organic chemistry unfolds around stars and how comets, asteroids and meteorites may have carried those ingredients to Earth.

items.title: [Resilience.org] Why pronatalism won’t reverse birth-rate decline
items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

Slower growth — and ultimately zero growth — is good for people and the planet, but not for those in power, who for millennia relied on women's reproductive labor to produce more workers, consumers, and taxpayers. Today’s Trump-era and even progressive pronatalist policies try to reverse the trend, but there's a reason why they won't work.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] AI flags more than 250,000 suspicious cancer research papers
items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

A powerful new AI tool has uncovered what could be one of the biggest integrity problems in modern science. After analyzing 2.6 million cancer research papers published between 1999 and 2024, researchers identified more than 250,000 studies with writing patterns resembling papers suspected of being produced by fraudulent "paper mills."

items.title: [ScienceDaily] New AI blood test predicts heart disease 15 years early
items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

A new AI-powered blood test could give people a remarkably early warning of serious heart and circulation problems. Developed by researchers at the University of Hong Kong, CardiOmicScore analyzes thousands of proteins and metabolites to estimate the risk of six major cardiovascular diseases, including heart attack, stroke, heart failure, and atrial fibrillation. Unlike genetic risk scores, which remain fixed throughout life, the system captures biological changes linked to a person’s current health, lifestyle, and environment.
items.summary: A new AI-powered blood test could give people a remarkably early warning of serious heart and circulation problems.
